Lineup classifier — training review

625 labeled frames (1097 total) · 56 misclassified · threshold 0.7 · trained 2026-07-24T06:05:38.458Z
dashed boxes = regions the classifier sees (yellow: left lane, orange: crosswalk)

In plain terms: a webcam photographs the ferry lineup every few minutes, and riders mark the moment cars back up past the crosswalk. From those examples the computer learns, for two fixed patches of road (the lane where the line builds, and the crosswalk itself), which spots being light or dark usually means the lineup has reached the crosswalk. For each new photo it adds up those learned clues into a confidence score between 0 and 1 — at 0.7 or higher it says “past crosswalk”. It is a deliberately simple learner: no deep network, just a weighted sum of pixels, small enough to run in milliseconds and to inspect by eye (see below).

For experts: binary logistic regression on raw pixel intensities. Preprocessing: two fixed fractional crops (left lane 48×27 + crosswalk 24×14) → grayscale, normalized to [0,1], concatenated — the same module (functions/lib/lineup-features.js) runs at training and inference, so there is no train/serve skew. Labels: per-sailing rider marks reduced latest-wins (lineup-labels.js, shared with the app); frames captured at/after the mark are positive. Optimizer: full-batch gradient descent, 300 epochs, lr 0.5, L2 0.0001. Split: ~80/20 by sailing (md5 bucket of the sailing key) since frames within a sailing are near-duplicates and a frame-level split would leak. Decision threshold 0.7; the model ships as JSON weights and runs in ~5 ms/frame on CPU inside the existing Cloud Function poll.

Predicted crosswalk times

Frames of each sailing are classified in capture order; the lineup is deemed past the crosswalk at the first positive frame confirmed by the next frame also being positive (a lone positive is treated as noise). Detected 65 of 126 sailings · mean |Δ| vs human tag: 4 min over 62 tagged sailings.

How the classifier works

The model never sees the whole photo: the two dashed regions are cropped, downscaled to grayscale grids (left lane 48×27 + crosswalk 24×14), concatenated, and each of the 1632 pixels gets one learned weight (logistic regression). A pixel's brightness × its weight is its vote; the votes plus a bias are summed and squashed to a probability. ≥ 0.7 means “past crosswalk”.


the learned weight maps (top: left lane, bottom: crosswalk) — red pixels vote “past crosswalk” when bright, blue pixels vote “not yet” when bright

The explain button on any card shows this frame's actual model input and which pixels decided its outcome.

ROI picker — draw tighter crop regions

Pick a region, then drag on the photo to draw its box (redraw to replace). The dashed boxes are the regions currently in use (yellow: left lane, orange: crosswalk). Copy the JSON and hand it to the classifier maintainer (fractions of the frame, same convention as REGIONS in functions/lib/lineup-features.js).

copied ✓

Why the model decided this

each panel stacks the two regions: left lane on top, crosswalk below.


what the model saw (crops → grayscale grids)


learned weights (same for every frame)


this frame's votes (input × weight)


votes − weights — where this frame falls short of a fully-bright region: strongest where an influential pixel is dark

red pushes toward “past crosswalk”, blue toward “not yet”; stronger color = stronger pull.
